title: Hardcoded API Keys or Secrets Detected
description: Scans page source for 60+ secret patterns across cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), payments (Stripe, PayPal, Square), AI services (OpenAI, Anthropic, HuggingFace), databases (MongoDB, PostgreSQL, Redis, Supabase), messaging (Slack, Discord, Twilio, Telegram), email (SendGrid, Mailgun, Mailchimp), CI/CD (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket), private keys (RSA, SSH, PGP), and generic API key/password assignments.
evidence: Live detection with partially-redacted key values as proof.
riskImpact: Exposed API keys allow attackers to access your third-party services, steal data, incur charges, and pivot into internal systems.
explanation: Hardcoded secrets in HTML/JavaScript are accessible to anyone viewing page source. Automated bots continuously scan the internet for exposed API keys and exploit them within minutes of exposure.
fixSteps: Immediately rotate all exposed keys and revoke old ones.
Move secrets to server-side environment variables.
Use a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, Doppler).
Add pre-commit hooks like git-secrets or truffleHog.
Audit your CI/CD pipeline for secret leaks.

codeExamples.label: Server-side only
codeExamples.code:
const apiKey = process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY;
// NEVER: const apiKey = 'sk_live_abc123...'

codeExamples.label: Next.js env
codeExamples.code:
# .env.local (never committed)
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_...
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-proj-...

# Only NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars are exposed to the browser

title: Stripe webhook signing secret in client bundle
description: Stripe whsec_* values must live server-side. If they show up in client code, attackers can forge webhook events.
evidence: Async / body / header check for secret-stripe-webhook-endpoint.
riskImpact: Move webhook secret to environment variables; never bundle it into client JS
explanation: Stripe whsec_* values must live server-side. If they show up in client code, attackers can forge webhook events.
fixSteps: Move webhook secret to environment variables; never bundle it into client JS

title: Google Maps API key in source
description: Google Maps API keys (AIzaSy*) in client code can be abused to bill against your account.
evidence: Async / body / header check for secret-google-maps-api-key.
riskImpact: Restrict by HTTP referrer in Google Cloud Console; consider server-side proxy
explanation: Google Maps API keys (AIzaSy*) in client code can be abused to bill against your account.
fixSteps: Restrict by HTTP referrer in Google Cloud Console; consider server-side proxy

title: Google OAuth client_secret in source
description: Google client_secret values must never appear in client-side code.
evidence: Async / body / header check for secret-google-oauth-client-secret.
riskImpact: Use the OAuth server-side flow; never ship the client_secret to the browser
explanation: Google client_secret values must never appear in client-side code.
fixSteps: Use the OAuth server-side flow; never ship the client_secret to the browser

title: Firebase API key (public) in source
description: Firebase Web API keys (AIzaSy*) are public by design. Security depends on Firestore rules / App Check.
evidence: Async / body / header check for secret-firebase-api-key-public.
riskImpact: Enable Firebase App Check; review Firestore / RTDB security rules
explanation: Firebase Web API keys (AIzaSy*) are public by design. Security depends on Firestore rules / App Check.
fixSteps: Enable Firebase App Check; review Firestore / RTDB security rules

explanation: AWS secret access keys (40-char base64) must never be in client code or public buckets.
fixSteps: Rotate the key in IAM immediately; move to short-lived STS credentials

title: PEM private key in source

title: JWT in client-side config
description: JWTs in source are at minimum replayable; if signed with HS256 and the secret is also leaked, attackers forge tokens.
evidence: Async / body / header check for secret-jwt-in-config.
riskImpact: Don't store JWTs in client config; use HttpOnly session cookies
explanation: JWTs in source are at minimum replayable; if signed with HS256 and the secret is also leaked, attackers forge tokens.
fixSteps: Don't store JWTs in client config; use HttpOnly session cookies

fixSteps: Rotate the API key in OCI console, revoke the user, and move config to instance principals.

fixSteps: Revoke via Settings -> Repository -> Deploy tokens; switch to CI/CD job tokens (per-job TTL).

fixSteps: Rotate immediately and switch to runner registration via group-runner auth-token workflow.
